coredata.dc:title: In situ formation of fluorescence species for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and organophosphorus pesticide via the ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of AgPd bimetallic nanoflowers
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Luo
Liu, Jing
Liu, Yani
Chen, Haoyu
Zhang, Youyu
Liu, Meiling
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection strategies for enzymes and their inhibitors not only have critical significance in clinical diagnosis but can be extended to food and environment analysis. Here, bimetallic AgPd alloy nanoflowers were synthesized using carbon dots as the reductant via a two-step reduction method. The AgPd bimetallic nanoflowers exhibit ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity, which can rapidly catalyze the oxidization of ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA). The DHA can then react with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce 3-(1, 2-dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ), accompanying with the characteristic fluorescence peak at 440 nm for DFQ. Alkaline phosphatase catalyzes l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate to generate AA, while organic phosphorus pesticides (OPPs) such as chlorpyrifos can inhibit the activity of alkaline phosphatase. By utilizing the enzyme-nanozyme tandem catalytic reaction, the fluorescence of the system varies in the presence of different concentrations of ALP or OPPs, which can be used to detect ALP in serum and OPPs in vegetables and fruits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
AgPd/C nanoflowers
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
Organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs)
Ascorbate oxidase (AAO)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots, Ag/C NCs and AgPd/C nanoflowers
Ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers
Procedures for assay of ascorbic acid
Detection procedures for detection of ALP
Detection of ALP in human serum and chlorpyrifos in real vegetable samples
Synthesis and characterization of AgPd/C nanoflowers
Ascorbate oxidase like activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers and the mechanism study
Detection of AA based on the ascorbate oxidase like activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers
Quantitative analysis of the ALP activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province
Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274047, 21974042, 21645008), Hunan Provincial Education Department Project (18A010), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30012). Human serum samples based procedures were under taken in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and were approved by the Biomedical Research Ethics Committee of Hunan Normal University. Informed consents were obtained from human participants of this study.

This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22274047 , 21974042 , 21645008 ), Hunan Provincial Education Department Project ( 18A010 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2021JJ30012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and the high-resolution TEM images (inset) of Ag/C NCs; (B) TEM and the high-resolution images (inset) of AgPd/C nanoflowers; (C) Corresponding EDX elemental mapping of Ag and Pd in AgPd/C nanoflowers; (D) SEM image of AgPd/C nanoflowers.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of AgPd/C nanoflowers; (B-D) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, Ag 3d and Pd 3d in AgPd/C nanoflowers.

UV–vis spectra of AA in the presence (A) or absence (B) of AgPd/C nanoflowers with the existence of O2; (C) Steady-state kinetic assay of the ascorbate oxidase like activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers: the solid circle is the experimental data, and the solid curve confirms to the Michaelis-Menten model. (D-G) The mass spectra of AA (D), AA in the presence of AgPd/C nanoflowers after reaction (E), AA and OPD in the absence (F) or presence (G) of AgPd/C nanoflowers after reaction.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the systems of AA + OPD (a), AgPd/C nanoflowers + AA + OPD (b), AgPd/C nanoflowers + OPD (c) and OPD (d) under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm). (B) Fluorescence spectra of the system OPD-AA-O2-AgPd/C nanoflowers with different concentrations of AA: 0.001 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 μM, (C) Calibration plot of (B) using the fluorescence at 440 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the system of OPD-AAP-O2-AgPd/C nanoflowers with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm of the system at different ALP concentrations: 0, 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.75, 5.00, 10.00 U/L; (B) Calibration plot of (A) using the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm; (C) Selectivity of the proposed method for ALP assay. The concentration of all substances is 5 mg/L.

(A) Effect of reaction time for the ALP and chlorpyrifos (with the concentration of 2.25 U/L and 500 μg/L for them, respectively) in the system; (B) Fluorescent response of the OPD-AAP-O2-AgPd/C nanoflowers in the presence of ALP with the concentration of 2.25 U/L with different concentrations of chlorpyrifos: 0, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500 μg/L; (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensities and the concentrations of chlorpyrifos in (B).
Schematic representation of the synthesis of AgPd/C nanoflowers and the fluorescence sensing platform for the sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase and organophosphorus pesticides.

head.ce:title: In situ formation of fluorescence species for the detection of alkaline phosphatase and organophosphorus pesticide via the ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of AgPd bimetallic nanoflowers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive detection strategies for enzymes and their inhibitors not only have critical significance in clinical diagnosis but can be extended to food and environment analysis. Here, bimetallic AgPd alloy nanoflowers were synthesized using carbon dots as the reductant via a two-step reduction method. The AgPd bimetallic nanoflowers exhibit ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity, which can rapidly catalyze the oxidization of ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA). The DHA can then react with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce 3-(1, 2-dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ), accompanying with the characteristic fluorescence peak at 440 nm for DFQ. Alkaline phosphatase catalyzes l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate to generate AA, while organic phosphorus pesticides (OPPs) such as chlorpyrifos can inhibit the activity of alkaline phosphatase. By utilizing the enzyme-nanozyme tandem catalytic reaction, the fluorescence of the system varies in the presence of different concentrations of ALP or OPPs, which can be used to detect ALP in serum and OPPs in vegetables and fruits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely found in various human organs, including the bone, liver, placenta, and intestine, which plays a crucial role in catalyzing the dephosphorylation of proteins, nucleic acids, and small molecules. The typical serum level of ALP, which ranges between 40 and 150 U/L (Song, Hong, Kwok, Lam, Liu, & Tang, 2014), serves as an essential diagnostic marker, as anomalies in ALP levels are often associated with various diseases, such as leukemia response, osteomalacia, lymphoma (Wiedemann, Charney, Barger, Schaeffer, & Kitchell, 2005), diabetes (Jin et al., 2019), and various types of cancer and liver tumors (Fernandez & Kidney, 2007). ALP is also used as a diagnostic biomarker for a few blood-related illnesses, such as Wilson’s disease, where low ALP levels are typically detected. In this regard, great efforts have been made to develop fast and convenient analytical methods for the detection of ALP activity including electrochemical strategies (Balbaied & Moore, 2019), UV–visible absorption (C. H. Zhang et al., 2022), fluorescence (X. Y. Zhang et al., 2020), chromatography (Hasegawa, Sugita, Takatani, Matsuura, Umemura, & Haraguchi, 2006), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Ingram, Moore, & Graham, 2009). Among these technologies, fluorescence analysis is a simple and sensitive method, and a large number of fluorescence sensors based on carbon dots (G. L. Li et al., 2016), semiconductor quantum dots (Freeman, Finder, Gill, & Willner, 2010), fluorescent organic compound (Hu, Zeng, Yu, & Wu, 2015; Liang, Kwok, Shi, Tang, & Liu, 2013), gold nanoclusters (Halawa, Gao, Saqib, Kitte, Wu, & Xu, 2017) have been developed in the determination of ALP. However, some of the sensing materials face issues such as harsh synthetic conditions, complex detection steps, or unclear mechanisms. Thus, it is crucial to develop acost-effective and simple approach to determine ALP activity that possesses sufficient sensitivity to detect the disease at an early stage.

The development of effective methods for ALP activity can also provide specific strategy for the detection of organophosphate pesticides (OPPs). It is known that the OPPs is widespread in agricultural because of their high effectiveness and relatively low persistence in the environment (Cai et al., 2020). However, improper use of these chemicals poses a serious threat to the ecological environment and human health due to resulting environmental pollution and food safety hazards (J. Li et al., 2023). OPPs inhibits acetylcholinesterase, which catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylcholine, a neurotransmitter in the central nervous system. Consequently, the transmission of nerve impulses is blocked (Hassani et al., 2017), leading to the extreme toxicity of OPPs and demanding the development of a rapid and ultra-sensitive detection method in real samples (Gai, Pu, Wang, Zhu, & Li, 2023). Since the activities of acetylcholinesterase (Cancar et al., 2016), ALP (Lv, Wei, Liu, Liu, Wei, & Liu, 2016), an organophosphate hydrolase (Mulchandani, Chen, Mulchandani, Wang, & Rogers, 2001) can be inhibited by organophosphorus pesticides, the fluorescence detection method for OPPs can be developed according to the enzyme inhibition process. Compared with some methods commonly used for pesticide detection, such as chemiluminescence strategy (Chang, Yu, Hou, Hu, & Li, 2023), portable electrochemical (X. J. Liu, Cheng, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2022), colorimetric (Gai et al., 2023), electrochemical strategy (Yu, Chang, Zhuang, Li, Hou, & Li, 2022), the fluorescence method shows significant analytical potential with its simple operation and satisfactory sensitivity. Based on this mechanism, the fluorescence detection methods for OPPs have been explored.

Ascorbate oxidase (AAO), a multi-ketone enzyme, can effectively catalyze the oxidation of AA to produce dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) and water (W. Zhang et al., 2018). However, the potential applicability of AAO in biomedicine, biosensor, and biotechnology is not yet fully addressed. Similar to some other natural enzymes, AAO has some drawbacks that hinder its practical use in sensing. These include low thermal stability, high cost, complicated preparation and purification process, and susceptibility to inactivation (Z. Wang et al., 2023). As a potential alternative for natural enzymes, nanozyme with intrinsic enzyme mimicking properties have increasingly attracted much attention owing to their exceptional advantages and widespread potential applications (Xu et al., 2021). Numerous nanomaterials, including metal oxides (Q. W. Liu, Zhang, Wang, Zhang, & Cui, 2021), metal–organic frameworks (Lan, Wan, & Wang, 2008), and metals and carbon-based nanomaterials (Yang et al., 2016), with various enzyme-mimicking activities, have been explored, such as oxidase, peroxidase, catalase, and glucose oxidase (Chen et al., 2016). Noble metal nanoparticles and their alloys, in particular, have gained popularity in various fields due to their exceptional optical and electronic properties, superior chemical stability, and excellent catalytic activity, making them ideal for biosensing, immunoassay, antibiotics, and anticancer therapy (Gao et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2023a). However, studies investigating the ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of nanomaterials are limited.

In this study, AgPd alloy nanoflowers with excellent AAO-like activity were synthesized utilizing carbon dots as the reductant. The AgPd alloy nanoflowers catalyzes AA into dehydroascorbic acid (DHA), which in turn reacts with OPD under certain conditions to yield 3-(1, 2-dihydroxyethyl)furo [3,4-b] quinoxalin-1(3H)-one (DFQ) with strong fluorescence at 440 nm due to the quinoxaline core skeleton fluorophore. The fluorescence intensity was found to be proportional to the concentration of AA thereby allowing for its quantification.In the presence of ALP, l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) will be catalyzed into AA, and hence, the system can be utilized for the quantitative of ALP activity. Due to the inhibitory effect of OPPs on ALP enzyme activity, OPPs can be further determined. This environmentally-friendly synthesis of AgPd/C nanoflowers with excellent AAO-like activity allows for the sensitive detection of ALP in serum and OPPs in vegetables. This novel idea combining enzyme-nanozyme catalysis with the in-situ formation of fluorescence species provides a specific strategy for the detection of various substances in food and environmental areas.

HCl, AgNO3, ethanediol, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), methanol, and ascorbic acid (AA) were all analytical reagents and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). PdCl2 was bought from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd. NaOH was purchased from Tianjin Hengxing chemical reagent manufacture Co., Ltd. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was obtained from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Sodium l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) was obtained from Beijing Biotopped Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) was obtained from Shanghai Dibai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Chlorpyrifos, malathion, and phoxim were bought from Tanmo Quality Inspection - Reference Material Center. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M) were prepared with K2HPO4·3H2O and KH2PO4. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray mapping spectroscopy (EDS mapping) were conducted on JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) and edax energy spectrometer (Octane SDD, America), respectively. The contents of Ag and Pd in AgPd/C nanoflowers were quantified by an inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 720ES, USA). UV–vis data were obtained via UV-2600 (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, British). The morphology of the material was characterized by JSM 7401 (JEOL, Japan) field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on the fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Mass spectrum (MS) data were obtained by Shimadzu LC-MS 8050 mass spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots, Ag/C NCs and AgPd/C nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were electrochemically synthesized based on our previous work (Lu et al., 2015), the hydroxyl-rich C-dots were prepared using ethylene glycol as the origin of carbon source by one-pot electrochemical method. Briefly, 0.8 g NaOH was dissolved in water and mixed with 80 mL ethylene glycol, electrolyzed under 30 V (DC) for 40 min with two platinum sheets acting as the positive and the negative electrodes. After electrolysis, the electrolyte solution was neutralized with HCl, dialyzed (Mw 3000) against water at room temperature for three days and then centrifugated for 10 min at 11000 rpm, and the supernatant was reserved and diluted to 8 mg/mL with water. The obtained C-dots solution could be stored at 4 °C for several months.

The Ag/C nanoparticles (Ag/C NCs) were synthesized according to our previous (Luo et al., 2023a). A certain volume of silver nitrate aqueous solution (10 mM) was added dropwise to 6 mL of the NaOH (10 mM) treated C-dots aqueous solution and stirred for 3 h under 30 °C in water bath. The color of the solution changed from dark brown to light yellow, indicating the formation of Ag/C NCs.

The AgPd/C nanoflowers were synthesized as follows: 6 mL of Ag/C NCs aqueous solution and 10 mL of AA aqueous solution (15 g/L) were added in turn, and 940 μL of palladium chloride solution (10 mM) was added within 5 min at 45 °C. After 10 min of rapid stirring, the color of the solution changed from brown to black. The resulting black product was carefully washed with ultrapure water several times and finally dried by vacuum freeze-drying to get black powder, which was then dispersed in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ascorbate oxidase mimetic activity of the AgPd/C nanoflowers, the catalytic oxidation of AA in the presence of O2 was performed. In detail, 50 μL of AgPd/C nanoflowers dispersion (0.05 mg/mL) was added to the mixture consisting of 500 μL (0.125 mM) of AA and 450 μL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 5.0). Immediately, the solution was transferred to the standard quartz cell and the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded at fixed time intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for assay of ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: For the sensitive detection of AA, the procedure is described as follows: 500 μL of OPD solution (20 mM) and 50 μL of AgPd/C nanoflowers dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) were added into 2.95 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH 5.5), and 500 μL of AA solution with different concentrations were added to the mixtures, incubated at 50 °C for 30 min before measurements. At last, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedures for detection of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: The typical procedure involves incubating 100 μL of ALP solution with different activities, 100 μL of AAP (10 mM), and 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.05 M, pH 7.4) at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, a mixture of 50 μL of the AgPd/C nanoflowers dispersion (0.1 mg/mL), 500 μL of OPD solution (20 mM), and 2.95 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 5.5) were added into the above mixture, followed by further incubation at 50 °C for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra are recorded with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

ALP was exposed to various concentrations of OPPs and incubated at 37 °C for 70 min. 100 μL of AAP (10 mM) and 250 μL of Tris-HCl (0.05 M, pH 7.4) were introduced. Afterwards, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. 50 μL of the AgPd/C nanoflowers dispersion (0.1 mg/mL), 500 μL of OPD solution (20 mM) and 2.95 mL PBS (0.1 M, pH 5.5) were added into the above mixture. Fluorescence spectra were collected in the range of 370–600 nm at room temperature. The inhibitory efficiency (%) of OPPs was calculated as follows:
Inhibition % = (F430-F430,OPPs)/F430×100%
Where F430 is the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm without OPPs, and F430,OPPs is the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm in the presence of OPPs inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP in human serum and chlorpyrifos in real vegetable samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples obtained from the hospital of Hunan Normal University were diluted for 50 times and detected using the standard additional methods by spiking of different concentrations of ALP (0, 0.4, 1, 5 U/L). The assay was performed similarly to the procedure of the assay of ALP mentioned above, except that 100 μL of the serum sample was used instead of 100 μL of ALP standard solution. All samples were assessed in triplicate.

To assess the practicality of the proposed technique for detecting chlorpyrifos in real samples, representative vegetables such as leeks and cucumbers were purchased from a local supermarket in Changsha, China. The edible parts of these vegetables were first diced and crushed before adding 5.0 g of samples to 25 mL of methanol. The mixture was then treated with a juicer for 20 min and centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 30 min. The supernatant was collected via a 0.22 μm filter and diluted 10 times, while adding different standard chlorpyrifos solution with the concentrations of 0, 5, 50, 400 μg/L to actual real samples. Finally, the proposed method was employed to determine the obtained samples. All samples underwent three parallel experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of AgPd/C nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of AgPd/C nanoflowers was illustrated in Scheme 1 A. Ag/C NCs were prepared by reducing silver nitrate with C-dots. PdCl2 solution was slowly dropped into Ag/C NCs dispersion to prepare the Ag-Pd nanocomposite through galvanic displacement method. The morphology of the as-prepared nanomaterials was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the synthesized Ag/C NCs have good dispersion, and the shape is basically spherical with the size less than 10 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.235 nm in the HRTEM image is associated to Ag. As shown in Fig. 1B, AgPd/C nanomaterial displayed as a flower-like structure, and the metal clusters are smaller than 50 nm in diameter with the individual nanoparticles size less than 10 nm. The morphology is greatly different from that of Ag/C NCs. The HRTEM image shows that the lattice spacing is 0.226 nm, assigned to the (111) planes of faces-centered cubic (fcc) of AgPd alloy, which was different from the (111) d-spacings of fcc Pd and fcc Ag with 0.22 nm and 0.24 nm (Z. L. Wang, Yan, Wang, Ping, & Jiang, 2013), respectively. This may suggest the formation of alloy structure. As shown in Fig. 1C, Ag and Pd elements were found clearly distributed, which also indicated the formation of alloy structure. And the elemental mapping analysis also confirmed the existence of the Ag and Pd in the nanoflowers. The EDX of AgPd/C nanoflowers also describes the main components of the material, as shown in Fig. S1A. SEM images of AgPd/C nanoflowers also show that the AgPd/C nanoflowers is of three-dimensional nanoflower morphology (Fig. 1D).

The formation of AgPd/C nanoflowers was further confirmed by UV spectra. Silver nanoparticles have an ultraviolet absorption peak at 400 nm, while palladium nanoparticles have an ultraviolet absorption peak at 260 nm (Luo et al., 2023b). As shown in Fig. S1B, Ag/C NCs have an ultraviolet absorption peak at about 400 nm. AgPd/C nanoflowers showed corresponding UV absorption peaks at 260 nm and 400 nm, respectively, which indicated that Ag and Pd nanoparticles were distributed in AgPd/C nanoflowers. XPS was carried out to confirm the elemental formation and chemical state of the prepared nanomaterials. Fig. 2 A shows that AgPd/C nanoflowers is mainly composed of C, O, Ag and Pd. Fig. 2B shows that C1s has three different peaks at 284.58, 286.38 and 288.58 eV, attributing to the carbon species adsorbed on the surface of AgPd nanoflowers, CO group and CO group, respectively (L. Zhang et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 2C, two peaks at 367.48 and 373.58 eV can be observed, which can be assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively, agreed well with the theoretic value of the metallic Ag (S. Zhang et al., 2017). This indicates that the AgPd/C nanoflowers contain Ag0 and Ag+. The XPS spectrum of Pd 3d (Fig. 2D) shows two chemical states of Pd on the surface of AgPd/C nanoflowers. The peaks at 335.18 eV and 337.98 eV of Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2 attributed to the metallic of Pd0. These results confirm the formation of palladium crystals on Ag/C NCs. While the peaks at 335.98 and 340.38 eV originated from Pd2+ (Z. L. Wang, Yan, Wang, Ping, & Jiang, 2013). However, for inorganic materials, the testing depth of XPS is only around 5–10 nm, and the surface carbon content of the material is more, resulting in lower peaks of Ag and Pd. The results may also suggest that some oxide species formed on the surface of Pd in the process of drying. The content of silver and palladium in the synthesized AgPd/C nanoflowers measured by ICP-OES (Table S1), given the contents of 20.9% and 48.2%, respectively, which was very close to the feed ratio of 3:7 in the material preparation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbate oxidase like activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers and the mechanism study
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbate oxidase can catalyze the oxidation of AA to produce water and dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) in the presence of oxygen. The UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 3 A) was used to monitor the oxidation of AA in the presence of dissolved O2, with AgPd/C nanoflowers acting as the catalyst. AA exhibits a distinct absorption maximum at 265 nm. However, when AgPd/C nanoflowers were incubated with AA, an obvious decrease in the peak of 265 nm was observed, demonstrating that AA was consumed. As depicted in Fig. 3A, AA can be completely oxidized within 10 min, as evidenced by the near disappearance of the characteristic peak at 265 nm. To confirm the role of oxygen, an experiment was also conducted under de-oxygenated condition. The results provided further support for theinvolvement of O2 in the reaction, as the absence ofO2 did not lead to a significant decrease in the absorbance at 265 nm, as shown in Fig. S2. At the same time, whenthe AgPd/C nanoflowers were absent, there was no considerable change observed in theabsorption peak at 265 nm (Fig. 3B), indicatingthat the oxidation of AA by O2 proceeded at a sluggish rate without the nanocatalyst. All these results provide evidence that the AgPd/C nanoflowers possess ascorbate oxidase like activity.

Mass spectrometry was employed to validatethe oxidation of AA, as depicted in Fig. 3D. When AgPd/C nanoflowers were absent, even after 10 min of incubation, the system only exhibited the characteristic mass spectrum peak at 177.15 corresponding to AA. However, in the presence of AgPd/C nanoflowers, an obvious peak at 175.05 emerged. Thisfinding suggests the conversion ofAA to DHA (Fig. 3E). Therefore, AgPd/C nanoflowersexhibit ascorbate oxidase like activity, effectivelyfacilitatingthe catalytic oxidation of AA to DHA.

To obtain the steady-state kinetic parameters, the catalytic behavior of AgPd/C nanoflowers with AA as substrate was studied based on the enzyme kinetics theory of the Michaelis–Menten equation, one of the most widely used models to study enzymatic reaction. The kinetic parameters of Km and Vmax are obtained by Michaelis-Menten equation. As shown in Table S2, compared with the kinetic parameters of AAO, AgPd/C nanoflowers have a higher Km, which indicates that the affinity of nanocomposites for AA not as good as natural AAO. However, the Vmax values are similar, indicating that AgPd/C nanoflowers has a considerable catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA based on the ascorbate oxidase like activity of AgPd/C nanoflowers
ce:section.ce:para: Under the catalysis of AgPd/C nanoflowers, AA is oxidized into DHA. DHA can react with OPD to produce fluorescent quinoxaline derivatives with the appearance of a peak at 440 nm. Moreover, AgPd/C nanoflowers cannot catalyze the oxidation of OPD in the presence of O2 even in 30 mins, as shown in Fig. S3. This indicate that AgPd/C nanoflowers do not have the properties of oxidase mimics, which will not affect the further reaction of OPD and DHA. Hence, the amount of AA can be determined by the fluorescence variations at 440 nm (Scheme 1B). As shown in Fig. 4 A, in the absence of AgPd/C nanoflowers, the fluorescence intensity of the AA and OPD reaction system is much lower than that in the presence of AgPd/C nanoflowers. This is mainly because AgPd/C nanoflowers can catalysis the oxidation of AA. In the absence of AgPd/C nanoflowers, large amount of non-oxidized AA in the system, and meanwhile, the OPD or AA has no fluorescence. Hence, the fluorescence is closely related to the resultant DHA in the system. Mass spectrometry was also used to confirm the mechanism. In the absence of AgPd/C nanoflowers, the characteristic peak of DFQ did not appear in the reaction system of OPD and AA (Fig. 3F). However, the characteristic mass spectrum peak of DFQ at 263.1 appeared with theintroductionof AgPd/C nanoflowers (Fig. 3G). This peak differs significantly from that of OPD, suggesting that the reaction of DHA and OPD led to the formation of DFQ. Therefore, based on the mechanism, AA can be selectively detected.

For efficient detection, the sensing conditions were optimized (Fig. S4). The incubation time of 30 min, incubation temperature of 50 ℃, pH of 5.5 (PBS), concentrations of OPD and AgPd/C nanoflowers of 0.625 mM and 0.1 mg/mL, respectively, were selected as the optimal conditions. Under the optimized conditions, as presented in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence of the system increases progressively with the concentration of AA. A good linear relationship can be found in the range of 0.1––25 μM (Fig. 4C), with the detection limit of 0.02 μM. Compared to some previous methods (Table S3), the proposed method displayed better performance. Due to the specific catalytic nanoenzymes and the in-situ formation of fluorescent products, this method can also eliminate the defect of poor selectivity for AA detection and realize the sensitive detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of the ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that AA is a typical catalysis product of ALP, the detection platform for AA can beutilized for the analysis of AA-related biological enzyme of ALP. As shown in Scheme 1B, ALPhas the ability to catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP, resulting in the formation of AA. This reaction allows for the development of a sensing platform for ALP activity. After the optimal concentration of AAP of 100 μM/L is determined in Fig. S5, ALP activity was assessed. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the concentration of ALP increased. This observationindicates that ALP effectivelycatalyzed the dephosphorylation of AAP, leading to the consistent production of AA. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity is proportional to ALP activity within the range of 0.125–10.00 U/L, given the linear regression equation of F = 645CALP + 192.5 (R2 = 0.998). The detection limit of this method for ALP was calculated to be 0.05 U/L using the 3σ/S method. This value is significantly lower than those of detection limits reportedin most other methods for ALP(Table S4), demonstrating the superiority of this method.The specificity of the developed assay for ALP is also evaluated (Fig. 5C). Some common proteases in blood were selected as potential interfering substances, such as glucose oxidase (GOX), lysozyme, trypsin, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), catalase (CAT), etc. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity for the ALP assay was much higher than these interferences even their concentrations were 100 times higher than that of ALP. This indicates that the detection of ALPis not affected by these substances due to the specific dephosphorylation of AAP to AA and the specific interaction between DHA and OPD. Currently, there are limited reports on the integration ofascorbate oxidase-like nanozyme-catalyzed reaction and the in-situ fluorescent product formation for the detection of ALP activity. This method also offers high sensitivityand is less affected by thesurrounding environment, thereby further enhancing itsselectivity. All these superiorities promise its potential in practical application.

To further assess the practical performance of the developed sensor, the activity of ALP in serum samples was monitored. As illustrated in Table S5, the recoveries for the biological samples are between 99.0% and 102.5% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.3–5.0%. The experimental results indicate the applicability of this assay for ALP analysis in real samples.

Phosphatase is an enzyme that can remove phosphate groups from a substrate through dephosphorylation. This process involves hydrolyzing the monophosphate to produce phosphate ions and free hydroxyl groups.OPPs are substrate analogues of alkaline phosphatase. As shown in Fig. S6, it is also a common inhibitor, which can inhibit the activity of alkaline phosphatase through the following mechanisms: Phosphate group binding of phosphatase activity site: Organophosphorus compound can be covalent bond bound to the phosphate group in the activity site of alkaline phosphatase. This binding will alter the conformation of the enzyme, thereby preventing substrate binding and catalytic reactions. Phosphate group binding at the substrate binding site of phosphatase: Organophosphorus compound can also bind covalent bond with the phosphate group in the substrate binding site of alkaline phosphatase. This binding will prevent substrate binding and hinder the progress of enzyme catalyzed reactions. Phosphate group binding of phosphatase activity site of enzyme activity site: Organophosphorus compound can also combine with other amino acid residues in alkaline phosphatase activity site by covalent bond, thus changing the conformation and activity of the enzyme. In conclusion, organic phosphorus inhibits the catalytic activity of alkaline phosphatase by covalent bond with the phosphate group or other amino acid residues in the active site of the enzyme. This inhibition mechanism can be reversible or irreversible, depending on the structure of organophosphorus compound and the way they bind to enzymes (Colovic, Krstic, Lazarevic-Pasti, Bondzic, & Vasic, 2013). To verify the feasibility of the constructed fluorescence platform for OPPs, chlorpyrifos was chosen as the representative target pesticide. The inhibitory effect of OPPs on the enzyme was observed to be significantly influenced by the incubation time. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased and reacheditsminimum at 70 min (Fig. 6 A). Therefore, 70 min was chosen as the optimal incubation reaction time for chlorpyrifos and ALP. Under the optimized conditions, variousconcentrations of chlorpyrifos were introduced into the system. As the concentration of chlorpyrifos increased, the activity of ALP was continuously inhibited, resulting ina reductionof AA and the formation of fluorescent resultant product, DFQ. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreased in the range of chlorpyrifos concentrations from 1 μg/L to 500 μg/L (Fig. 6B). A linearcorrelation relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of chlorpyrifos was established, with the standard regression equation for chlorpyrifos being F = 1.667C + 1623 (R2 = 0.999), asillustrated in Fig. 6C. The calculated detection limit for chlorpyrifos was 0.56 μg/L by 3σ/S. Additionally, the platform was alsosuccessfully employed for the detection of other OPPs, includingphoxim and malathion, as depicted in Fig. S7. The results demonstratethat the AgPd/C nanoflowers-system can serveas a universal platform for OPPs. Moreover,inhibitory effect of the chlorpyrifos to ALP was found to be more pronounced compared toother OPPs. This observation suggests that different organophosphorus pesticides exhibit varying levels of inhibitory effects on ALP.

The method described above was also applied to analyzethe presence ofchlorpyrifos leek and cucumber samples. The results presented in Table S6 demonstrate that the recoveries of chlorpyrifos in the samples were 88.00–105.18%. Additionally, the relative standard deviations were below1.2%. These findings indicate that the accuracy of this method is satisfactory.

The synthesis of AgPd/C nanoflowers was achieved by utilizing the reducibility of carbon dots in the presence of silver salts. The AgPd/C nanoflowers exhibit exceptional ascorbate oxidase-like activity, enabling efficient catalysis of AA into DHA in the presence of O2. Based on this, a highly selective and sensitivefluorescence platform was developed for the detection of AA, ALP and organophosphorus pesticides. The enzyme-nanozyme tandem catalytic reactionsuccessfully enabled the sensitive detection of ALP and chlorpyrifos,while also demonstrating good selectivity towards enzymes and their inhibitors.Thisintegration ofnanozyme catalyzed reactions and in-situ formation of fluorescent products provides an effective strategy for analysis, which can be further extended to construct other sensing platforms for food and environment analysis.

Luo Luo: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis. Jing Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yani Liu: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Haoyu Chen: Methodology. Youyu Zhang: Resources, Supervision. Meiling Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shouzhuo Yao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274047, 21974042, 21645008), Hunan Provincial Education Department Project (18A010), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30012).

Human serum samples based procedures were under taken in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and were approved by the Biomedical Research Ethics Committee of Hunan Normal University. Informed consents were obtained from human participants of this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137062.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
